{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Takeo Onsen (武雄温泉) | image = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg | Comment = Takeo Onsen Roumon (武雄温泉楼門) | Location = {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市}} | Access = {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線}} – approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅}} | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}} | Spring Temperature = 45 - 51°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 17 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}}).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of {{ill|facial lotion|ja|化粧水}}.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by {{ill|Tatsuno Kingo|ja|辰野金吾}}, with a celebration of their completion tier 2]][[Category: held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館}} and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記}}'', compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the {{ill|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign|ja|文禄・慶長の役}} (the Korean invasions) under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the {{ill|Nagasaki Kaidō|ja|長崎街道}}, known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that {{ill|Jingū-hōō|ja|神功皇后}} pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏}}, and in the early Edo period, {{ill|Date Masamune|ja|伊達政宗}} and {{ill|Miyamoto Musashi|ja|宮本武蔵}}, while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|ja|吉田松陰}} are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅}} on the {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線}} and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本}}.

The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記}}'', compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the {{ill|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign|ja|文禄・慶長の役}} (the Korean invasions) under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the {{ill|Nagasaki Kaidō|ja|長崎街道}}, known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that {{ill|Jingū-hōō|ja|神功皇后}} pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏}}, and in the early Edo period, {{ill|Date Masamune|ja|伊達政宗}} and {{ill|Miyamoto Musashi|ja|宮本武蔵}}, while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|ja|吉田松陰}} are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅}} on the {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線}} and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hot Spring Rankings|ja|温泉番付|1=Onsen rankings}} * {{ill|Soapland|ja|ソープランド}} * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

revision.text:
{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Takeo Onsen (武雄温泉) | image = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg | Comment = Takeo Onsen Roumon (武雄温泉楼門) | Location = {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市}} | Access = {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線}} – approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅}} | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}} | Spring Temperature = 45 - 51°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 17 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}}).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of {{ill|facial lotion|ja|化粧水}}.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by {{ill|Tatsuno Kingo|ja|辰野金吾}}, with a celebration of their completion tier 2]][[Category: held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館}} and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記}}'', compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the {{ill|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign|ja|文禄・慶長の役}} (the Korean invasions) under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the {{ill|Nagasaki Kaidō|ja|長崎街道}}, known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that {{ill|Jingū-hōō|ja|神功皇后}} pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏}}, and in the early Edo period, {{ill|Date Masamune|ja|伊達政宗}} and {{ill|Miyamoto Musashi|ja|宮本武蔵}}, while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|ja|吉田松陰}} are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅}} on the {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線}} and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hot Spring Rankings|ja|温泉番付|1=Onsen rankings}} * {{ill|Soapland|ja|ソープランド}} * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記}}'', compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the {{ill|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign|ja|文禄・慶長の役}} (the Korean invasions) under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the {{ill|Nagasaki Kaidō|ja|長崎街道}}, known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that {{ill|Jingū-hōō|ja|神功皇后}} pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏}}, and in the early Edo period, {{ill|Date Masamune|ja|伊達政宗}} and {{ill|Miyamoto Musashi|ja|宮本武蔵}}, while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|ja|吉田松陰}} are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅}} on the {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線}} and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hot Spring Rankings|ja|温泉番付|1=Onsen rankings}} * {{ill|Soapland|ja|ソープランド}} * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Takeo Onsen (武雄温泉) | image = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg | Comment = Takeo Onsen Roumon (武雄温泉楼門) | Location = {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市}} | Access = {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線}} – approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅}} | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}} | Spring Temperature = 45 - 51°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 17 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国}}).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of {{ill|facial lotion|ja|化粧水}}.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞}} (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by {{ill|Tatsuno Kingo|ja|辰野金吾}}, with a celebration of their completion tier 2]][[Category: held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館}} and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記}}'', compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the {{ill|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign|ja|文禄・慶長の役}} (the Korean invasions) under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the {{ill|Nagasaki Kaidō|ja|長崎街道}}, known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that {{ill|Jingū-hōō|ja|神功皇后}} pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏}}, and in the early Edo period, {{ill|Date Masamune|ja|伊達政宗}} and {{ill|Miyamoto Musashi|ja|宮本武蔵}}, while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|ja|吉田松陰}} are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅}} on the {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線}} and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hot Spring Rankings|ja|温泉番付|1=Onsen rankings}} * {{ill|Soapland|ja|ソープランド}} * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記}}'', compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏}} advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the {{ill|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign|ja|文禄・慶長の役}} (the Korean invasions) under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the {{ill|Nagasaki Kaidō|ja|長崎街道}}, known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that {{ill|Jingū-hōō|ja|神功皇后}} pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏}}, and in the early Edo period, {{ill|Date Masamune|ja|伊達政宗}} and {{ill|Miyamoto Musashi|ja|宮本武蔵}}, while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|ja|吉田松陰}} are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅}} on the {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線}} and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hot Spring Rankings|ja|温泉番付|1=Onsen rankings}} * {{ill|Soapland|ja|ソープランド}} * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市|12=draft}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国|12=draft}}).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=draft}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=draft}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|12=draft}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of {{ill|facial lotion|ja|化粧水|12=draft}}.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|12=draft}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|12=draft}} (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by {{ill|Tatsuno Kingo|ja|辰野金吾|12=draft}}, with a celebration of their completion tier 2]]}} <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.takeo-kk.net/ {{ill|Takeo City Tourism Association|ja|武雄市観光協会}]]

'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市|12=draft}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国|12=draft}}).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=draft}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=draft}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|12=draft}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of {{ill|facial lotion|ja|化粧水|12=draft}}.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|12=draft}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|12=draft}} (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by {{ill|Tatsuno Kingo|ja|辰野金吾|12=draft}}, with a celebration of their completion tier 2]]}} <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.takeo-kk.net/ {{ill|Takeo City Tourism Association|ja|武雄市観光協会}]]
<!--Takeo Onsen-->
{{Onsen}}
{{draft categories| [[Category: held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|12=draft}} and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|12=draft}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|12=draft}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|12=draft}}'', compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft}}. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|12=draft}} advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|12=draft}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the {{ill|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign|ja|文禄・慶長の役|12=draft}} (the Korean invasions) under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft}}, the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the {{ill|Nagasaki Kaidō|ja|長崎街道|12=draft}}, known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|12=draft}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that {{ill|Jingū-hōō|ja|神功皇后|12=draft}} pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|12=draft}}, and in the early Edo period, {{ill|Date Masamune|ja|伊達政宗|12=draft}} and {{ill|Miyamoto Musashi|ja|宮本武蔵|12=draft}}, while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|ja|吉田松陰|12=draft}} are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=draft}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線|12=draft}} and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|12=draft}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hot Spring Rankings|ja|温泉番付|1=Onsen rankings|12=draft}} * {{ill|Soapland|ja|ソープランド|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=draft}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|12=draft}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|12=draft}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

revision.text:
{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Takeo Onsen (武雄温泉) | image = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg | Comment = Takeo Onsen Roumon (武雄温泉楼門) | Location = {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市|12=draft|12=draft}} | Access = {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線|12=draft|12=draft}} – approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅|12=draft|12=draft}} | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=draft|12=draft}} | Spring Temperature = 45 - 51°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 17 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市|12=draft|12=draft}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国|12=draft|12=draft}}).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=draft|12=draft}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|12=draft|12=draft}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of {{ill|facial lotion|ja|化粧水|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|12=draft|12=draft}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|12=draft|12=draft}} (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by {{ill|Tatsuno Kingo|ja|辰野金吾|12=draft|12=draft}}, with a celebration of their completion tier 2]]}} <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.takeo-kk.net/ {{ill|Takeo City Tourism Association|ja|武雄市観光協会}]]
{{Onsen}}
{{draft categories|
}}
{{draft categories| [[Category: held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|12=draft|12=draft}} and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|12=draft|12=draft}}'', compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|12=draft|12=draft}} advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|12=draft|12=draft}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the {{ill|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign|ja|文禄・慶長の役|12=draft|12=draft}} (the Korean invasions) under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft|12=draft}}, the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the {{ill|Nagasaki Kaidō|ja|長崎街道|12=draft|12=draft}}, known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|12=draft|12=draft}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that {{ill|Jingū-hōō|ja|神功皇后|12=draft|12=draft}} pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, and in the early Edo period, {{ill|Date Masamune|ja|伊達政宗|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Miyamoto Musashi|ja|宮本武蔵|12=draft|12=draft}}, while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|ja|吉田松陰|12=draft|12=draft}} are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=draft|12=draft}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hot Spring Rankings|ja|温泉番付|1=Onsen rankings|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Soapland|ja|ソープランド|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=draft|12=draft}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|12=draft|12=draft}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.
== References == {{Footnote Help}} {{Wiki Portal Link|Hot Springs|[[File:Hot springs 001.svg|34px|Portal: 温泉]] [[Category:pages to be reviewed for curly bracket replacement error]] [[Category:Hot Springs in Saga Prefecture]] [[Category:Takeo City]] [[Category:Translated Pages]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:Legends]] [[Category:Onsen Origin Myth]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }}

In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|12=draft|12=draft}}'', compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|12=draft|12=draft}} advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|12=draft|12=draft}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the {{ill|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign|ja|文禄・慶長の役|12=draft|12=draft}} (the Korean invasions) under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft|12=draft}}, the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the {{ill|Nagasaki Kaidō|ja|長崎街道|12=draft|12=draft}}, known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|12=draft|12=draft}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that {{ill|Jingū-hōō|ja|神功皇后|12=draft|12=draft}} pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, and in the early Edo period, {{ill|Date Masamune|ja|伊達政宗|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Miyamoto Musashi|ja|宮本武蔵|12=draft|12=draft}}, while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|ja|吉田松陰|12=draft|12=draft}} are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=draft|12=draft}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hot Spring Rankings|ja|温泉番付|1=Onsen rankings|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Soapland|ja|ソープランド|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=draft|12=draft}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|12=draft|12=draft}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Takeo Onsen (武雄温泉) | image = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg | Comment = Takeo Onsen Roumon (武雄温泉楼門) | Location = {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市|12=draft|12=draft}} | Access = {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線|12=draft|12=draft}} – approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅|12=draft|12=draft}} | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=draft|12=draft}} | Spring Temperature = 45 - 51°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 17 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市|12=draft|12=draft}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国|12=draft|12=draft}}).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=draft|12=draft}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|12=draft|12=draft}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of {{ill|facial lotion|ja|化粧水|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|12=draft|12=draft}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|12=draft|12=draft}} (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by {{ill|Tatsuno Kingo|ja|辰野金吾|12=draft|12=draft}}, with a celebration of their Completion tier 2]]}} <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.takeo-kk.net/ {{ill|Takeo City Tourism Association|ja|武雄市観光協会}]]
{{Onsen}}

}}
{{draft categories| [[Category: held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|12=draft|12=draft}} and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).

In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.

{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Takeo Onsen (武雄温泉) | image = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg | Comment = Takeo Onsen Roumon (武雄温泉楼門) | Location = {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市|12=draft|12=draft}} | Access = {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線|12=draft|12=draft}} – approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅|12=draft|12=draft}} | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=draft|12=draft}} | Spring Temperature = 45 - 51°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 17 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市|12=draft|12=draft}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国|12=draft|12=draft}}).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|12=draft|12=draft}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|12=draft|12=draft}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of {{ill|facial lotion|ja|化粧水|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|12=draft|12=draft}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|12=draft|12=draft}} (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by {{ill|Tatsuno Kingo|ja|辰野金吾|12=draft|12=draft}}, with a celebration of their Completion tier 2]]}} <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.takeo-kk.net/ {{ill|Takeo City Tourism Association|ja|武雄市観光協会}]]
{{Onsen}}
{{draft categories|
}}
{{draft categories| [[Category: held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|12=draft|12=draft}} and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|12=draft|12=draft}}'', compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|12=draft|12=draft}} advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|12=draft|12=draft}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the {{ill|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign|ja|文禄・慶長の役|12=draft|12=draft}} (the Korean invasions) under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|12=draft|12=draft}}, the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the {{ill|Nagasaki Kaidō|ja|長崎街道|12=draft|12=draft}}, known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|12=draft|12=draft}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that {{ill|Jingū-hōō|ja|神功皇后|12=draft|12=draft}} pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|12=draft|12=draft}}, and in the early Edo period, {{ill|Date Masamune|ja|伊達政宗|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Miyamoto Musashi|ja|宮本武蔵|12=draft|12=draft}}, while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|ja|吉田松陰|12=draft|12=draft}} are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=draft|12=draft}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅|12=draft|12=draft}} on the {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|12=draft|12=draft}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hot Spring Rankings|ja|温泉番付|1=Onsen Rankings|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Soapland|ja|ソープランド|12=draft|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|12=draft|12=draft}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|12=draft|12=draft}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|12=draft|12=draft}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2015年5月}} {{日本の温泉地 |名称 = 武雄温泉 |画像 = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg |コメント = 武雄温泉楼門 |所在地 = [[佐賀県]][[武雄市]] |交通アクセス = [[佐世保線]][[武雄温泉駅]]より徒歩約10分 |泉質 = [[単純温泉]]、[[炭酸水素塩泉]] |泉温 = 45 - 51 |湧出量 = |pH = |液性の分類 = |浸透圧の分類 = |宿泊施設数 = 17 |総収容人員数 = |年間浴客数 = |統計年度 = |外部リンク = [http://www.takeo-kk.net/ 武雄市観光協会] |特記事項 = }}
'''武雄温泉'''（たけおおんせん）は、[[佐賀県]][[武雄市]]（旧国[[肥前国]]）にある[[温泉街]]である。
== 泉質 == * [[単純温泉]] * [[炭酸水素塩泉]] ** [[源泉]][[温度]]45 - 51℃ 温泉水を利用した[[化粧水]]も製造されている。
== 温泉街 == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|楼門から見た元湯（左）と新館（右）]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|新館]] [[武雄温泉楼門]]は、新館と共に国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されており、温泉街のシンボルである<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">渡部正隆(2015年4月15日). “楼門と新館創建100周年を祝賀 武雄”. [[毎日新聞]] (毎日新聞社)</ref>。 両者とも[[1914年]]（[[大正]]3年）4月12日に完成祝賀会が開かれた[[辰野金吾]]の設計によるものである<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" />。 門を入ると、資料館である[[武雄温泉新館]]と後述する共同浴場及び貸切風呂がある。新館は[[1973年]]（[[昭和]]48年）までは共同浴場であった。[[2003年]]（[[平成]]15年）3月に新館が復元され、楼門とともに[[2005年]]（平成17年）7月22日に重要文化財に指定された。
共同浴場は3軒存在する。 *元湯 *蓬莱湯 *鷺乃湯 の3軒である。
また、[[鍋島氏]]専用の浴場であった施設が存在し、貸切風呂として入浴できる。 *殿様湯 *家老湯 である。貸切風呂は他にも柄崎亭が存在する。
旅館は17軒存在する。
温泉街では春と夏に祭がある。また、周辺には窯元も多く、毎月陶器市が開催される。
== 歴史 == 武雄温泉に関する最古の史料は、[[奈良時代]]に編纂された『[[肥前国風土記]]』である。『肥前国風土記』には「郡の西に温泉の出る巌（いわ）有り、岸峻しくて人跡（ひと）牢（まれ）に及（いた）る也」とあり<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=武雄市史 上巻 |year=1972 |publisher=武雄市史編纂委員会 |page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=武雄市図書館・歴史資料館 平成15年特別企画展「温泉～和みの空間～」 |year=2003 |publisher=武雄市図書館・歴史資料館（エポカル武雄） |page=11}}</ref>、奈良時代では温泉があったものの岩山の崖が険しく人はまれにしか行かなかった<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=発掘された日本列島2005展・地域展「肥前国 風土記の世界」 |year=2007 |publisher=佐賀県立博物館編、佐賀県芸術文化育成基金発行}}</ref>とある。
戦国時代､[[龍造寺氏]]が西肥前へ進攻する際､[[龍造寺長信]]が武雄温泉の利用に関与していたことが知られている<ref>中村知裕｢中世九州の温泉と権力・交通｣(『七隈史学』9号､2008年)</ref>｡
[[豊臣秀吉]]が[[文禄・慶長の役|朝鮮出兵]]の際に、負傷兵士の[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]として利用したといわれる。
[[江戸時代]]は[[長崎街道]]の[[宿場町]]・塚崎宿としても栄えた。江戸時代前期に成立した『肥前古跡縁起』によると、[[神功皇后]]が太刀の柄（つか）で岩の割れ目をこじ開けたところ湯が流れ出たため柄崎温泉と名付けられたとの伝説も生まれた<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|和書 |title=図説 武雄・鹿島・嬉野・杵島・藤津の歴史 |year=2009 |publisher=株式会社郷土出版社 |pages=44-45}}</ref>。幕末には長崎を往来した勤皇志士や文人らが盛んにこの湯を訪れたという。著名な人物を挙げると、佐賀藩城主の鍋島氏、[[江戸時代]]初期には[[伊達政宗]]、[[宮本武蔵]]が、また[[幕末]]には[[フィリップ・フランツ・フォン・シーボルト|シーボルト]]、[[吉田松陰]]らが入湯したといわれる。
戦後は[[嬉野温泉]]と共に歓楽温泉としての道を進んだこともあったが、今日ではその傾向は薄い。その落ち着いた静かな佇まいから、お忍びで訪れる芸能人などの著名人も多いと言われている。最近は観光地としての開発が盛んで、様々な観光施設やモニュメントが設けられている。
== アクセス == * [[佐世保線]]・ [[西九州新幹線]][[武雄温泉駅]]より徒歩約10分。
== 関連項目 == * [[温泉番付]] * [[ソープランド]] * [[嬉野温泉]]…塚崎宿の次の宿場町 * [[塩田津]]（[[重要伝統的建造物群保存地区]]）…嬉野宿の次の宿場町
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{ウィキポータルリンク|温泉|[[画像:Hot springs 001.svg|34px|Portal:温泉]]}} <references />
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.takeo-kk.net/ 武雄市観光協会]

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2015年5月}} {{日本の温泉地 |名称 = 武雄温泉 |画像 = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg |コメント = 武雄温泉楼門 |所在地 = [[佐賀県]][[武雄市]] |交通アクセス = [[佐世保線]][[武雄温泉駅]]より徒歩約10分 |泉質 = [[単純温泉]]、[[炭酸水素塩泉]] |泉温 = 45 - 51 |湧出量 = |pH = |液性の分類 = |浸透圧の分類 = |宿泊施設数 = 17 |総収容人員数 = |年間浴客数 = |統計年度 = |外部リンク = [http://www.takeo-kk.net/ 武雄市観光協会] |特記事項 = }}
'''武雄温泉'''（たけおおんせん）は、[[佐賀県]][[武雄市]]（旧国[[肥前国]]）にある[[温泉街]]である。
== 泉質 == * [[単純温泉]] * [[炭酸水素塩泉]] ** [[源泉]][[温度]]45 - 51℃ 温泉水を利用した[[化粧水]]も製造されている。
== 温泉街 == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|楼門から見た元湯（左）と新館（右）]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|新館]] [[武雄温泉楼門]]は、新館と共に国の[[重要文化財]]に指定されており、温泉街のシンボルである<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">渡部正隆(2015年4月15日). “楼門と新館創建100周年を祝賀 武雄”. [[毎日新聞]] (毎日新聞社)</ref>。 両者とも[[1914年]]（[[大正]]3年）4月12日に完成祝賀会が開かれた[[辰野金吾]]の設計によるものである<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" />。 門を入ると、資料館である[[武雄温泉新館]]と後述する共同浴場及び貸切風呂がある。新館は[[1973年]]（[[昭和]]48年）までは共同浴場であった。[[2003年]]（[[平成]]15年）3月に新館が復元され、楼門とともに[[2005年]]（平成17年）7月22日に重要文化財に指定された。
共同浴場は3軒存在する。 *元湯 *蓬莱湯 *鷺乃湯 の3軒である。
また、[[鍋島氏]]専用の浴場であった施設が存在し、貸切風呂として入浴できる。 *殿様湯 *家老湯 である。貸切風呂は他にも柄崎亭が存在する。
旅館は17軒存在する。
温泉街では春と夏に祭がある。また、周辺には窯元も多く、毎月陶器市が開催される。
== 歴史 == 武雄温泉に関する最古の史料は、[[奈良時代]]に編纂された『[[肥前国風土記]]』である。『肥前国風土記』には「郡の西に温泉の出る巌（いわ）有り、岸峻しくて人跡（ひと）牢（まれ）に及（いた）る也」とあり<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=武雄市史 上巻 |year=1972 |publisher=武雄市史編纂委員会 |page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=武雄市図書館・歴史資料館 平成15年特別企画展「温泉～和みの空間～」 |year=2003 |publisher=武雄市図書館・歴史資料館（エポカル武雄） |page=11}}</ref>、奈良時代では温泉があったものの岩山の崖が険しく人はまれにしか行かなかった<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=発掘された日本列島2005展・地域展「肥前国 風土記の世界」 |year=2007 |publisher=佐賀県立博物館編、佐賀県芸術文化育成基金発行}}</ref>とある。
戦国時代､[[龍造寺氏]]が西肥前へ進攻する際､[[龍造寺長信]]が武雄温泉の利用に関与していたことが知られている<ref>中村知裕｢中世九州の温泉と権力・交通｣(『七隈史学』9号､2008年)</ref>｡
[[豊臣秀吉]]が[[文禄・慶長の役|朝鮮出兵]]の際に、負傷兵士の[[湯治#湯治場|湯治場]]として利用したといわれる。
[[江戸時代]]は[[長崎街道]]の[[宿場町]]・塚崎宿としても栄えた。江戸時代前期に成立した『肥前古跡縁起』によると、[[神功皇后]]が太刀の柄（つか）で岩の割れ目をこじ開けたところ湯が流れ出たため柄崎温泉と名付けられたとの伝説も生まれた<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|和書 |title=図説 武雄・鹿島・嬉野・杵島・藤津の歴史 |year=2009 |publisher=株式会社郷土出版社 |pages=44-45}}</ref>。幕末には長崎を往来した勤皇志士や文人らが盛んにこの湯を訪れたという。著名な人物を挙げると、佐賀藩城主の鍋島氏、[[江戸時代]]初期には[[伊達政宗]]、[[宮本武蔵]]が、また[[幕末]]には[[フィリップ・フランツ・フォン・シーボルト|シーボルト]]、[[吉田松陰]]らが入湯したといわれる。
戦後は[[嬉野温泉]]と共に歓楽温泉としての道を進んだこともあったが、今日ではその傾向は薄い。その落ち着いた静かな佇まいから、お忍びで訪れる芸能人などの著名人も多いと言われている。最近は観光地としての開発が盛んで、様々な観光施設やモニュメントが設けられている。
== アクセス == * [[佐世保線]]・ [[西九州新幹線]][[武雄温泉駅]]より徒歩約10分。
== 関連項目 == * [[温泉番付]] * [[ソープランド]] * [[嬉野温泉]]…塚崎宿の次の宿場町 * [[塩田津]]（[[重要伝統的建造物群保存地区]]）…嬉野宿の次の宿場町

{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Takeo Onsen (武雄温泉) | image = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg | Comment = Takeo Onsen Roumon (武雄温泉楼門) | Location = {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Access = {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Spring Temperature = 45 - 51°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 17 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of {{ill|facial lotion|ja|化粧水|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|qq=draft|qq=draft}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by {{ill|Tatsuno Kingo|ja|辰野金吾|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with a celebration of their Completion tier 2]]}} <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.takeo-kk.net/ {{ill|Takeo City Tourism Association|ja|武雄市観光協会}]]
{{Onsen}}
{{draft categories|
}}
{{draft categories| [[Category: held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the {{ill|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign|ja|文禄・慶長の役|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (the Korean invasions) under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the {{ill|Nagasaki Kaidō|ja|長崎街道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that {{ill|Jingū-hōō|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and in the early Edo period, {{ill|Date Masamune|ja|伊達政宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Miyamoto Musashi|ja|宮本武蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|ja|吉田松陰|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hot Spring Rankings|ja|温泉番付|1=Onsen Rankings|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Soapland|ja|ソープランド|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

Both structures were designed by {{ill|Tatsuno Kingo|ja|辰野金吾|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with a celebration of their Completion tier 2]]}} <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.takeo-kk.net/ {{ill|Takeo City Tourism Association|ja|武雄市観光協会}]]
{{Onsen}}
and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the {{ill|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign|ja|文禄・慶長の役|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (the Korean invasions) under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the {{ill|Nagasaki Kaidō|ja|長崎街道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that {{ill|Jingū-hōō|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and in the early Edo period, {{ill|Date Masamune|ja|伊達政宗|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Miyamoto Musashi|ja|宮本武蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|ja|吉田松陰|qq=draft|qq=draft}} are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Hot Spring Rankings|ja|温泉番付|1=Onsen Rankings|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Soapland|ja|ソープランド|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.
