{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Takeo Onsen (武雄温泉) | image = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg | Comment = Takeo Onsen Roumon (武雄温泉楼門) | Location = {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Access = {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Spring Temperature = 45 - 51°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 17 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of {{ill|facial lotion|ja|化粧水|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|qq=draft|qq=draft}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by {{ill|Tatsuno Kingo|ja|辰野金吾|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with a celebration of their completion. held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|qq=draft}}
and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|qq=draft}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|qq=draft}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.

'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in {{ill|Saga Prefecture|ja|佐賀県|qq=draft|qq=draft}} {{ill|Takeo City|ja|武雄市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (formerly part of {{ill|Hizen Province|ja|肥前国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of {{ill|facial lotion|ja|化粧水|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|qq=draft|qq=draft}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. {{ill|Mainichi Shimbun|ja|毎日新聞|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by {{ill|Tatsuno Kingo|ja|辰野金吾|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with a celebration of their completion. held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|qq=draft}}
and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|qq=draft}}, which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|qq=draft}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|qq=draft}}'', compiled during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代|qq=draft}}. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the {{ill|Ryūzōji clan|ja|龍造寺氏|qq=draft}} advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|qq=draft}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the {{ill|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign|ja|文禄・慶長の役|qq=draft}} (the Korean invasions) under {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|ja|豊臣秀吉|qq=draft}}, the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the {{ill|Nagasaki Kaidō|ja|長崎街道|qq=draft}}, known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|qq=draft}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that {{ill|Jingū-hōō|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft}} pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the {{ill|Nabeshima clan|ja|鍋島氏|qq=draft}}, and in the early Edo period, {{ill|Date Masamune|ja|伊達政宗|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Miyamoto Musashi|ja|宮本武蔵|qq=draft}}, while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Yoshida Shōin|ja|吉田松陰|qq=draft}} are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from {{ill|Takeo Onsen Station|ja|武雄温泉駅|qq=draft}} on the {{ill|Sasebo Line|ja|佐世保線|qq=draft}} and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Onsen Rankings|ja|温泉番付|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Soapland|ja|ソープランド|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|qq=draft}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.takeo-kk.net/ {{ill|Takeo City Tourism Association|ja|武雄市観光協会}]]

== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Fudoki|qq=}}'', compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzōji Naganobu|qq=}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the [[:en:Imjin War|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign]] (the Korean invasions) under [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the [[:en:Nagasaki Kaidō|Nagasaki Kaidō]], known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Koshiki Engi|qq=}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that [[:en:Empress Jingū|Jingū-hōō]] pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], and in the early Edo period, [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]], while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philipp Franz von Siebold|qq=}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kyushu|qq=}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Onsen Rankings|ja|温泉番付|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Rankings|qq=}} * [[:en:Types of prostitution in modern Japan|Soapland]] * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiota-tsu|qq=}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|qq=}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

revision.text:
{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Takeo Onsen (武雄温泉) | image = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg | Comment = Takeo Onsen Roumon (武雄温泉楼門) | Location = [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]] [[:en:Takeo, Saga|Takeo City]] | Access = [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] – approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Hot Spring|qq=}} | Spring Temperature = 45 - 51°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 17 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]] [[:en:Takeo, Saga|Takeo City]] (formerly part of [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]]).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Source Water Temperature|qq=}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of [[:en:Lotion|facial lotion]].
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Roumon|qq=}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by [[:en:Tatsuno Kingo|Tatsuno Kingo]], with a celebration of their completion. held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Shinkan|qq=}}
and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karazaki-tei|qq=}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Fudoki|qq=}}'', compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzōji Naganobu|qq=}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the [[:en:Imjin War|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign]] (the Korean invasions) under [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the [[:en:Nagasaki Kaidō|Nagasaki Kaidō]], known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Koshiki Engi|qq=}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that [[:en:Empress Jingū|Jingū-hōō]] pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], and in the early Edo period, [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]], while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philipp Franz von Siebold|qq=}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kyushu|qq=}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Onsen Rankings|ja|温泉番付|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Rankings|qq=}} * [[:en:Types of prostitution in modern Japan|Soapland]] * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiota-tsu|qq=}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|qq=}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

revision.text:
{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Takeo Onsen (武雄温泉) | image = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg | Comment = Takeo Onsen Roumon (武雄温泉楼門) | Location = [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]] [[:en:Takeo, Saga|Takeo City]] | Access = [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] – approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Hot Spring|qq=}} | Spring Temperature = 45 - 51°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 17 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]] [[:en:Takeo, Saga|Takeo City]] (formerly part of [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]]).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Source Water Temperature|qq=}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of [[:en:Lotion|facial lotion]].
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Roumon|qq=}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by [[:en:Tatsuno Kingo|Tatsuno Kingo]], with a celebration of their completion. held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Shinkan|qq=}}
and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karazaki-tei|qq=}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Fudoki|qq=}}'', compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzōji Naganobu|qq=}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the [[:en:Imjin War|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign]] (the Korean invasions) under [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the [[:en:Nagasaki Kaidō|Nagasaki Kaidō]], known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Koshiki Engi|qq=}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that [[:en:Empress Jingū|Jingū-hōō]] pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], and in the early Edo period, [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]], while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philipp Franz von Siebold|qq=}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kyushu|qq=}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Onsen Rankings|ja|温泉番付|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Rankings|qq=}} * [[:en:Types of prostitution in modern Japan|Soapland]] * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiota-tsu|qq=}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|qq=}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.
== References == <references />
== External Links == * [http://www.takeo-kk.net/ {{ill|Takeo City Tourism Association|ja|武雄市観光協会}]]
{{Onsen}}
{{Wiki Portal Link|Hot Springs|[[File:Hot springs 001.svg|34px|Portal: 温泉]]
}}
{{translated page|ja|武雄温泉|version=99946163|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Notable figures include the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], and in the early Edo period, [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]], while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philipp Franz von Siebold|qq=}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kyushu|qq=}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Onsen Rankings|ja|温泉番付|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Rankings|qq=}} * [[:en:Types of prostitution in modern Japan|Soapland]] * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiota-tsu|qq=}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|qq=}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

{{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Takeo Onsen (武雄温泉) | image = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg | Comment = Takeo Onsen Roumon (武雄温泉楼門) | Location = [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]] [[:en:Takeo, Saga|Takeo City]] | Access = [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] – approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Hot Spring|qq=}} | Spring Temperature = 45 - 51°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 17 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]] [[:en:Takeo, Saga|Takeo City]] (formerly part of [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]]).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Source Water Temperature|qq=}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of [[:en:Lotion|facial lotion]].
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Roumon|qq=}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by [[:en:Tatsuno Kingo|Tatsuno Kingo]], with a celebration of their completion. held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Shinkan|qq=}}
and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karazaki-tei|qq=}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Fudoki|qq=}}'', compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzōji Naganobu|qq=}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the [[:en:Imjin War|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign]] (the Korean invasions) under [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the [[:en:Nagasaki Kaidō|Nagasaki Kaidō]], known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Koshiki Engi|qq=}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that [[:en:Empress Jingū|Jingū-hōō]] pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], and in the early Edo period, [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]], while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philipp Franz von Siebold|qq=}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kyushu|qq=}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Onsen Rankings|ja|温泉番付|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Rankings|qq=}} * [[:en:Types of prostitution in modern Japan|Soapland]] * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiota-tsu|qq=}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|qq=}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]] [[:en:Takeo, Saga|Takeo City]] (formerly part of [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]]).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Source Water Temperature|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of [[:en:Lotion|facial lotion]].
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Roumon|qq=}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by [[:en:Tatsuno Kingo|Tatsuno Kingo]], with a celebration of their completion. held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Shinkan|qq=}}
and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karazaki-tei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Fudoki|qq=}}'', compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzōji Naganobu|qq=}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the [[:en:Imjin War|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign]] (the Korean invasions) under [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the [[:en:Nagasaki Kaidō|Nagasaki Kaidō]], known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Koshiki Engi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that [[:en:Empress Jingū|Jingū-hōō]] pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], and in the early Edo period, [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]], while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philipp Franz von Siebold|qq=}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kyushu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 西日本旅客鉄道}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Onsen Rankings|ja|温泉番付|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Rankings|qq=}} * [[:en:Types of prostitution in modern Japan|Soapland]] * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiota-tsu|qq=}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|qq=}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Koshiki Engi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that [[:en:Empress Jingū|Jingū-hōō]] pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], and in the early Edo period, [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]], while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philipp Franz von Siebold|qq=}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kyushu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 西日本旅客鉄道}}.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11546632]] Wikidata name: Takeo Onsen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:47, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Takeo Onsen (武雄温泉) | image = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg | Comment = Takeo Onsen Roumon (武雄温泉楼門) | Location = [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]] [[:en:Takeo, Saga|Takeo City]] | Access = [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] – approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Hot Spring|qq=|1=hydrogen carbonate spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567142}} | Spring Temperature = 45 - 51°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 17 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]] [[:en:Takeo, Saga|Takeo City]] (formerly part of [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]]).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Source Water Temperature|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of [[:en:Lotion|facial lotion]].
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Roumon|qq=|1=Takeo Onsen Tower Gate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96323705}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by [[:en:Tatsuno Kingo|Tatsuno Kingo]], with a celebration of their completion. held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Shinkan|qq=|1=Takeo Onsen Shinkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96323719}}
and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karazaki-tei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Fudoki|qq=|1=Hizen no Kuni Fudoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q111890610}}'', compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzōji Naganobu|qq=|1=Ryūzōji Naganobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11679403}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the [[:en:Imjin War|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign]] (the Korean invasions) under [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the [[:en:Nagasaki Kaidō|Nagasaki Kaidō]], known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Koshiki Engi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that [[:en:Empress Jingū|Jingū-hōō]] pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], and in the early Edo period, [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]], while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philipp Franz von Siebold|qq=|1=Siebold|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1961155}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kyushu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 西日本旅客鉄道|1=West Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q502125}}.

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11546632]] Wikidata name: Takeo Onsen [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:47, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Source Citation|date=May 2015}} {{infobox Onsen | Name = Takeo Onsen (武雄温泉) | image = Takeoonsen rohmon.jpg | Comment = Takeo Onsen Roumon (武雄温泉楼門) | Location = [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]] [[:en:Takeo, Saga|Takeo City]] | Access = [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] – approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] | Spring Quality = {{ill|Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}}, {{ill|Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate Hot Spring|qq=|1=hydrogen carbonate spring|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11567142}} | Spring Temperature = 45 - 51°C | Flow Rate = | pH = | Classification of Liquid Nature = | Classification of Osmotic Pressure = | Number of Lodging Facilities = 17 | Total Capacity = | Annual Number of Bathers = | Statistical Year = | Special Notes = }}
'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]] [[:en:Takeo, Saga|Takeo City]] (formerly part of [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]]).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Source Water Temperature|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of [[:en:Lotion|facial lotion]].
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Roumon|qq=|1=Takeo Onsen Tower Gate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96323705}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by [[:en:Tatsuno Kingo|Tatsuno Kingo]], with a celebration of their completion. held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Shinkan|qq=|1=Takeo Onsen Shinkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96323719}}
and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karazaki-tei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Fudoki|qq=|1=Hizen no Kuni Fudoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q111890610}}'', compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzōji Naganobu|qq=|1=Ryūzōji Naganobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11679403}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the [[:en:Imjin War|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign]] (the Korean invasions) under [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the [[:en:Nagasaki Kaidō|Nagasaki Kaidō]], known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Koshiki Engi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that [[:en:Empress Jingū|Jingū-hōō]] pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], and in the early Edo period, [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]], while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philipp Franz von Siebold|qq=|1=Siebold|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1961155}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kyushu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 西日本旅客鉄道|1=West Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q502125}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Onsen Rankings|ja|温泉番付|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Rankings|qq=|1=onsen banzuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562960}} * [[:en:Types of prostitution in modern Japan|Soapland]] * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiota-tsu|qq=|1=Shiotatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600983}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|qq=|1=Important Preservation Districts for Groups of Traditional Buildings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q850649}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.
== References == <references />

'''Takeo Onsen''' (武雄温泉) is an onsen town located in [[:en:Saga Prefecture|Saga Prefecture]] [[:en:Takeo, Saga|Takeo City]] (formerly part of [[:en:Hizen Province|Hizen Province]]).
== Spring Quality == * {{ill|Simple Hot Springs|lt=Simple Hot Spring|ja|単純温泉|1=Simple Hot Springs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Simple Hot Springs|qq=}} * {{ill|Bicarbonate springs|lt=Bicarbonate Hot Spring|ja|炭酸水素塩泉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bicarbonate springs|qq=}} ** {{ill|Source Water Temperature|ja|源泉温度|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Source Water Temperature|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} 45 - 51°C Hot spring water is also used in the manufacture of [[:en:Lotion|facial lotion]].
== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Roumon|qq=|1=Takeo Onsen Tower Gate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96323705}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by [[:en:Tatsuno Kingo|Tatsuno Kingo]], with a celebration of their completion. held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Shinkan|qq=|1=Takeo Onsen Shinkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96323719}}
and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karazaki-tei|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Fudoki|qq=|1=Hizen no Kuni Fudoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q111890610}}'', compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzōji Naganobu|qq=|1=Ryūzōji Naganobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11679403}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the [[:en:Imjin War|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign]] (the Korean invasions) under [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the [[:en:Nagasaki Kaidō|Nagasaki Kaidō]], known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Koshiki Engi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that [[:en:Empress Jingū|Jingū-hōō]] pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], and in the early Edo period, [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]], while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philipp Franz von Siebold|qq=|1=Siebold|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1961155}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kyushu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 西日本旅客鉄道|1=West Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q502125}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Onsen Rankings|ja|温泉番付|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Rankings|qq=|1=onsen banzuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562960}} * [[:en:Types of prostitution in modern Japan|Soapland]] * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiota-tsu|qq=|1=Shiotatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600983}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|qq=|1=Important Preservation Districts for Groups of Traditional Buildings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q850649}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.

== Onsen Town == [[File:Takeo Onsen Motoyu and Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|The original hot spring (left) and the new building (right) as seen from the Roumon]] [[File:Takeo Onsen Shinkan.jpg|thumb|200px|New Building]] {{ill|Takeo Onsen Roumon|ja|武雄温泉楼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Roumon|1=Takeo Onsen Tower Gate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96323705}} has been designated as an Important Cultural Property by the national government together with the new building, and serves as a symbol of the onsen town.<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15">Watanabe Masataka (April 15, 2015). “Celebrating the 100th Anniversary of the Construction of the Roumon and the New Building at Takeo Onsen”. [[:en:Mainichi Shimbun|Mainichi Shimbun]] (Mainichi Newspapers)</ref>. Both structures were designed by [[:en:Tatsuno Kingo|Tatsuno Kingo]], with a celebration of their completion. held on April 12, 1914 (Taishō 3).<ref name="mainichi-np-2015-4-15" /> Upon entering through the gate, visitors find the museum {{ill|Takeo Onsen Shinkan|ja|武雄温泉新館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeo Onsen Shinkan|1=Takeo Onsen Shinkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q96323719}}
and, as described later, both communal baths and private baths. The new building functioned as a communal bath until 1973 (Shōwa 48). In March 2003 (Heisei 15), the new building was restored, and together with the Roumon, they were designated as Important Cultural Properties on July 22, 2005 (Heisei 17).
There are three communal baths: * Motoyu * Hōraiyu * Saginoyu
In addition, there are facilities that were once reserved exclusively for the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], which now operate as private baths: * Tono-yu * Karo-yu There is also another private bath at {{ill|Karazaki-tei|ja|柄崎亭|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Karazaki-tei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
Seventeen ryokan are in operation.
The onsen town holds festivals in spring and summer. In addition, there are many pottery kilns in the surrounding area, and a pottery market is held every month.
== History == The oldest historical document concerning Takeo Onsen is the ''{{ill|Hizen Fudoki|ja|肥前国風土記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Fudoki|1=Hizen no Kuni Fudoki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q111890610}}'', compiled during the [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]]. The Hizen Fudoki states, “To the west of the district there is a rock from which hot spring water emerges; its cliffs are steep, and few people can reach it.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City History, Volume I|year=1972|publisher=Takeo City History Compilation Committee|page=647}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Takeo City Library & History Museum Special Exhibition “Onsen ~ A Space of Relaxation ~”|year=2003|publisher=Takeo City Library & History Museum (Epcor Takeo)|page=11}}</ref> Although a hot spring existed in the Nara period, the precipitous rocky cliffs meant that few people visited.<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated Exhibition “Excavated Japan 2005 – The World of Fudoki in Hizen Province”|year=2007|publisher=Saga Prefectural Museum, Saga Prefectural Arts and Culture Promotion Fund|pages=}}</ref>
In the Sengoku period, when the [[:en:Ryūzōji clan|Ryūzōji clan]] advanced into western Hizen, {{ill|Ryūzōji Naganobu|ja|龍造寺長信|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ryūzōji Naganobu|1=Ryūzōji Naganobu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11679403}} was known to have utilized Takeo Onsen.<ref>Chihiro Nakamura, “Onsen, Power, and Transportation in Medieval Kyushu” (Shichikuma Shigaku, No. 9, 2008)</ref>
It is said that during the [[:en:Imjin War|Bunroku-Keicho Campaign]] (the Korean invasions) under [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]], the onsen was used as a therapeutic bathing facility for wounded soldiers.
During the Edo period, Takeo Onsen flourished as a post town along the [[:en:Nagasaki Kaidō|Nagasaki Kaidō]], known as Tsukazaki-juku.
According to the ''{{ill|Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja|肥前古跡縁起|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hizen Koshiki Engi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'', compiled in the early Edo period, there is a legend that [[:en:Empress Jingū|Jingū-hōō]] pried open a fissure in a rock with the hilt of her sword, causing hot spring water to flow out, and thus naming it Karazaki Onsen.<ref name=":02">{{Cite book|Japanese|title=Illustrated History of Takeo, Kashima, Ureshino, Kijima, and Fujitsu|year=2009|publisher=Kyodo Publishing Co.|pages=44-45}}</ref> In the Bakumatsu period, loyalist samurai and literati traveling to and from Nagasaki frequented this hot spring. Notable figures include the [[:en:Nabeshima clan|Nabeshima clan]], and in the early Edo period, [[:en:Date Masamune|Date Masamune]] and [[:en:Miyamoto Musashi|Miyamoto Musashi]], while during Bakumatsu, {{ill|Philipp Franz von Siebold|ja|シーボルト|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Philipp Franz von Siebold|1=Siebold|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1961155}} and [[:en:Yoshida Shōin|Yoshida Shōin]] are said to have bathed here.
After the war, Takeo Onsen once developed as a pleasure hot spring along with {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}}, but that trend has since faded. Owing to its calm and quiet atmosphere, it is said that many celebrities visit discreetly. Recently, development as a tourist destination has progressed, with various tourist facilities and monuments being established.
== Access == * Approximately a 10‐minute walk from [[:en:Takeo-Onsen Station|Takeo Onsen Station]] on the [[:en:Sasebo Line|Sasebo Line]] and {{ill|JR Kyushu|ja|JR西日本|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/JR Kyushu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 西日本旅客鉄道|1=West Japan Railway Company|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q502125}}.
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Onsen Rankings|ja|温泉番付|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen Rankings|1=onsen banzuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562960}} * [[:en:Types of prostitution in modern Japan|Soapland]] * {{ill|Ureshino Onsen|ja|嬉野温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ureshino Onsen|qq=}} … the next post town following Tsukazaki-juku. * {{ill|Shiota-tsu|ja|塩田津|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiota-tsu|1=Shiotatsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109600983}} ({{ill|Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|ja|重要伝統的建造物群保存地区|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Important Traditional Buildings Preservation District|1=Important Preservation Districts for Groups of Traditional Buildings|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q850649}}) … the next post town following Ureshino-juku.
